data.title: Japan Offering Y2.4T Of 1.0% 5-Year Govt Bonds
data.description:
TOKYO--Japan's Ministry of Finance said Thursday it is auctioning Y2.4 trillion of five-year government bonds.

 
Issue                   5-year govt bond (No 178) 
Coupon                                       1.0% 
Settlement Date                      Apr 11, 2025 
Maturity Date                        Mar 20, 2030 
Offer Amount                        Y2.4 Trillion

This content was automatically published based on data and/or text from the original source. For feedback, write to singaporeeditors@dowjones.com.

data.title: Indian financial markets closed on April 10 for holiday
data.description:
India's equity, currency and debt markets will be closed on Thursday, April 10, for a local holiday. Trading will resume on Friday, April 11.

The NSE Nifty 50 fell 0.61% while the BSE Sensex declined 0.51% on Wednesday, tracking a global selloff sparked by U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs. A widely-expected rate cut and dovish stance by the RBI failed to lift sentiment.

The Indian rupee dropped 0.5% versus the U.S. dollar to 86.6875, tracking weakness in the Chinese yuan as sweeping U.S. tariffs came into effect, including a 104% levy on China, and fuelled worries of a slowdown in global growth.

The benchmark 10-year bond (IN067934G=CC) was quoted at 102.4325 rupees, with the yield down 3 bps at 6.4432%, its lowest since December 2021, after the Reserve Bank of India's dovish monetary policy decision and guidance triggered fresh investor purchases.

data.title: China lowers yuan midpoint fixing for sixth straight day to fresh 19-month low
data.description:
China lowered its official yuan midpoint for the six straight day to a fresh 19-month low against the dollar on Thursday.

Prior to market opening, the People's Bank of China (PBOC) set the midpoint rate , around which the yuan is allowed to trade in a 2% band, at 7.2092 per dollar, the weakest since September 11, 2023 but 1,392 pips firmer than a Reuters' estimate of 7.3484.

The PBOC has slightly loosened its grip on the currency this week by allowing official guidance to weaken past the key threshold of 7.2 per dollar level, although it came in much stronger than market projections, in what traders and analysts interpreted as an official attempt to keep the yuan steady.

The PBOC's focus on steady yuan moves comes even as the worsening U.S. trade war severely challenges the competitiveness of China's massive export sector, suggesting financial market stability remains the priority.

data.title: Taiwan Stocks Surge After President Trump's 90-Day Tariff PauseMarket Talk
data.description: Taiwan stocks surge after President Trump announced a 90-day pause on higher U.S. tariffs for most countries, ending a three-session heavy selloff. The tariff pause offers relief for investors as the island's export-dependent economy is highly exposed to the U.S. TSMC, the world's largest contract chip maker, which counts Nvidia and Apple as major clients, and Foxconn Technology, a major Apple supplier, are each up nearly 10%, the daily limit permitted in the Taiwan market. Quanta Computer and MediaTek gain 9.8% and 9.7%, respectively. Taiwan's benchmark Taiex index is 9.3% higher at 19012.03. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Posco Future M Target Price Cut to KRW140,000 From KRW330,000 by Daiwa Capital >003670.SE
Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

2131 ET - Taiwan stocks surge after President Trump announced a 90-day pause on higher U.S. tariffs for most countries, ending a three-session heavy selloff. The tariff pause offers relief for investors as the island's export-dependent economy is highly exposed to the U.S. TSMC, the world's largest contract chip maker, which counts Nvidia and Apple as major clients, and Foxconn Technology, a major Apple supplier, are each up nearly 10%, the daily limit permitted in the Taiwan market. Quanta Computer and MediaTek gain 9.8% and 9.7%, respectively. Taiwan's benchmark Taiex index is 9.3% higher at 19012.03. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2120 ET - Catapult Sports International's bull at Bell Potter sees the positives outweighing the negatives despite uncertainty over the potential impact of tariffs on the sports-tech provider. Analyst Chris Savage tells clients in a note that he anticipates a strong annual result, with annual contract value rising 19% on-year to US$103 million. He is also positive that Catapult can continue to deliver on cross-selling, especially with new product launches on the way. Savage points out that tariffs will only impact Catapult's hardware business, which is relatively small. Other than the Australian dollar, all currencies in which Catapult does business have held up well against the U.S. dollar, he adds. Bell Potter keeps a buy rating and A$4.00 target price on the stock, which is up 11% at A$3.35. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2113 ET - Singapore's FTSE Straits Times Index climbs 6.6% to 3617.89 in early trade, tracking Wall Street's rally overnight. President Trump's announcement of a 90-day pause in some of the U.S. reciprocal tariffs on the world has caused a "market that has been under extreme pressure for the past week to explode higher," Phillip Securities Research team says in a commentary. Among the best performers on the benchmark index, SATS Ltd. rises 8.5%, index heavyweight DBS adds 8.2%, and Mapletree Logistics Trust is 8.6% higher. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2105 ET - Malaysia's benchmark Kuala Lumpur Composite Index is 4.5% higher at 1464.19. The index is supported by Wall Street's rally and bargain hunting after recent losses, Apex Securities says in a note. Easing tariff concerns could lift sentiment in the near term and investors may target beaten-down stocks, with attention on coming U.S. inflation data for rate cues, the brokerage reckons. Sectors like technology and banking could lead gains on renewed risk appetite, while the energy sector may benefit from the oil price rebound, it adds. Among gainers, Axiata Group adds 4.6% and Gamuda Bhd. is 10% higher. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2104 ET - Santos Chair Keith Spence will seek to calm shareholder nerves at the Australian energy company's annual general meeting, following a 15% drop its in stock price this month. In prepared remarks, Spence says he believes Santos's stock is undervalued, and has been for some time, but that the company is strong and resilient and he is optimistic about its future. Still, "the last week has added a new layer of complexity," he says. "The global trading system that was based on building open, competitive markets over many decades following World War Two" has been upended. Santos is up 4.8% at A$5.595 amid a broadbased rally in global stocks after President Trump decided to pause steep tariffs on nearly 100 nations. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2056 ET - Goodman Group's bulls at Citi think the stock is attractively priced ahead of market updates that could act as share-price catalysts. Keeping a buy rating on the stock, Citi's analysts see the stock's recent underperformance relative to peers as a result of a capital raise and worries over the growth outlook for data centers. This has left the stock trading on cheaper multiples prior to its move into data centers, and offering value relative to global peers. The Citi analysts remain optimistic on the underlying demand both for data centers and for Goodman's core industrial business. They are watching for next month's 3Q update and August's FY results announcement. Citi keeps a A$40.00 target price on the stock, which is up 11% at A$29.01. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2048 ET - The scrutiny under which Australian supermarkets currently find themselves is likely to stop them from raising fees for so-called click-and-collect services any time soon, according to Citi analyst Adrian Lemme. Comparing Australian operators with global peers, he thinks that click-and-collect charges are a viable option for the likes of Coles and Woolworths to support earnings. The charge would partly recover the service cost, incentivize higher-margin in-store shopping, and enable price reinvestment, he writes in a note to clients. However, public and regulatory scrutiny over pricing practices and market share make near-term moves unlikely, he adds. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2031 ET - Peabody could abandon or renegotiate a deal to buy Anglo American's steelmaking coal operations if a fire at one of the mines it is buying is deemed a "material adverse change" under their agreement, Jefferies analyst Christopher LaFemina says in a note. "If it does indeed qualify, it is possible that Peabody could walk away or the two parties could revise the terms of the transaction," says LaFemina. This is a positive for Peabody, and a negative for Anglo American, which could possibly face a new takeover bid from rival BHP if the deal falls apart, he says. "In light of current market conditions and the uncertain market outlook, the consensus view is that Peabody would be best off if it could walk as the risks and the implied valuation are too high," says LaFemina. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2028 ET - South Korea's benchmark Kospi rises 5.0% to 2408.61 in early trade amid U.S. tariff relief. President Trump's 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for trading partners, except China, is sparking a broad-based market rally. Foreign and institutional investors are net buyers. Index heavyweight Samsung Electronics climbs 5.1%. Memory-chip maker SK Hynix jumps 12%, leading gains in local semiconductor stocks. Carmaker Hyundai Motor advances 7.9%. The won is strengthening against the greenback, with USD/KRW 1.9% lower at 1,455.65 in Seoul onshore trading. South Korea's 10-year government bond yield is up 4.6 bps at 2.732%.(kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

2029 ET - Australia's S&P/ASX 200 gains almost 6% in early trade as buyers piled into global equities following the Trump administration's move to pause some tariffs. The benchmark index was up 5.85% after about 20 minutes of Thursday's session, with all but two stocks at least 1% higher. Tech and energy stocks are leading the gains, with Block's Australia-listed stock, payment provider Zip, and data-center operator DigiCo surging by between 16% and 19%. All 11 sectors of the ASX 200 are in positive territory, even as some economists warned that continued tariff uncertainty may continue to weigh on markets. The ASX can exit its correction if it holds onto its early gains. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2022 ET - President Trump's announcement of 90-day pause on higher U.S. tariffs for most countries "pulls stocks and the market from the edge of the cliff," Wedbush analysts say in a note. "This was the news we and everyone on the Street was waiting for," the analysts say. "Now we would expect massive negotiations across the board over the coming months including China being front and center as the biggest wild card," the analysts say. China is still the largest "X variable" related to Apple and the broader supply chain, they add. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2017 ET - Japanese stocks are higher in early trade as fears about U.S. tariffs ease following President Trump's pause for so-called reciprocal tariffs. Bank and electronics stocks are leading gains. USD/JPY is at 147.28, up sharply from 145.38 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock-market close. The 10-year Japanese government bond yield is up 11 basis points at 1.380%. Investors are closely watching any responses from China after Trump also said he would raise levies on Chinese imports to 125%. The Nikkei Stock Average is up 8.4% at 34368.61. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

data.title: PBOC sets USD/ CNY mid-point today at 7.2092 (vs. estimate at 7.3484)
data.description:
The People's Bank of China (PBOC), China's central bank, is responsible for setting the daily midpoint of the yuan (also known as renminbi or RMB). The PBOC follows a managed floating exchange rate system that allows the value of the yuan to fluctuate within a certain range, called a "band," around a central reference rate, or "midpoint." It's currently at +/- 2%.

The previous close was 7.3445

data.title: GOP Holdouts Force Delay in Vote on Trump Budget Plan
data.description:
By Richard Rubin and Olivia Beavers

WASHINGTON — Republican leaders postponed a vote on the blueprint for President Trump's "one big, beautiful bill," throwing the GOP legislative agenda into temporary uncertainty as they try to find a path forward on tax cuts and spending.

A handful of hard-right conservatives resisted the pleas from Republican leaders and Trump, who had urged wavering House members to "close your eyes and get there." The holdouts argued that the plan didn't lock in nearly enough spending cuts alongside extensions of expiring tax cuts.

Three times earlier this year, Trump had been able to sway just enough House members on close votes, getting Mike Johnson (R., La.) re-elected as speaker, maneuvering a spending bill through Congress, and passing the first version of a budget. This time around, the playbook hasn't worked yet.

Key members of that group met with top Republican senators and then with Johnson before the vote for more than an hour, sessions that didn't produce enough of a breakthrough to get the budget adopted.

Johnson said late Wednesday that a vote could come Thursday, in advance of a scheduled two-week congressional recess.

Republican leaders are regrouping as they try to find a path forward. GOP lawmakers are largely united around extending expiring tax cuts, adding new tax cuts and boosting spending for border security and national defense. But they are at odds over budget deficits and spending cuts, and Wednesday's delayed vote might force them to confront the divides between the House and Senate that they have been sidestepping until now.

"I support the president. I trust the president," said Rep. Andy Ogles (R., Tenn.). "I do not trust the Senate. I've seen this movie. I know how it ends. Why are we going down this path? It's like Lucy and the football. She pulls it away every time."

The Senate's fiscal framework allows more than $5 trillion in tax cuts and locks in $4 billion in spending cuts. Senators say they are aiming for $2 trillion in spending cuts but wanted to allow maximum flexibility. Trump posted on social media on Tuesday that he favors major spending cuts, "hopefully" over $1 trillion.

The House version, passed in February without a single vote to spare, allows $4 trillion in tax cuts and requires $1.5 trillion in spending cuts, largely from the committee that handles Medicaid. The House's tax cuts could get larger if spending cuts get larger. Those same spending cuts could imperil the Senate coalition because members such as Sens. Josh Hawley (R., Mo.) and Lisa Murkowski (R., Alaska) are concerned about Medicaid.

The House budget would allow for a $4 trillion debt limit increase; the Senate version allows $5 trillion. The U.S. is likely to run out of ability to pay all its bills by August or September without a debt-limit increase, and that deadline might become the deadline for the big tax-and-spending bill.

This week's framework contains the House and Senate numbers, but the Senate figures are more important. The House can effectively change its numbers later with a simple-majority vote while the Senate would need 60 votes in a chamber that Republicans control 53-47.

Lawmakers who object to the Senate plan, including Reps. Lloyd Smucker (R., Pa.), David Schweikert (R., Ariz.) and Chip Roy (R., Texas), say they want to lock in minimum levels of significant spending cuts in writing now. They argue that party leaders' and senators' assurances about cuts coming later in the actual tax-and-spending bill aren't good enough.

"We're going to end up on the short end of the stick," Roy said on the House floor, warning that a final bill from the Senate would increase budget deficits. "Show me the math. At some point, if you're in Congress, pass a math test. Because the Senate bill doesn't add up."

Republicans have a 220-213 House majority, so they can lose only three members on any vote, if all Democrats are opposed. Democrats say the Trump agenda would cut spending on healthcare for Americans to fund tax cuts for the wealthy.

"He wants all of you guys to close your eyes and kiss his ass," Rep. Jim McGovern (D., Mass.) said Wednesday. "And I'm pretty sure that you're all about to do just that. What an embarrassment."

Republican leaders contend that the framework is merely the first step and that GOP lawmakers can object later if a final bill doesn't reduce enough spending.

"You want to stop now and trigger the largest tax increase in American history? Or do you want to keep moving? This is the process bill," said Rep. Tom Cole (R., Okla.), chairman of the House Appropriations Committee. "You should wait and see what the final bill is before you make a call."

In the past, Trump has swayed wavering members, calling the House cloakroom during votes to move the final few holdouts. He posted on social media Wednesday urging Republicans to get on board, and party leaders are pushing for action fast.

Smucker said he has told party leaders that he can't vote for the Senate plan unless there are changes to the text.

"You have to put guardrails in place, in writing, at the beginning of the process," he said.

Write to Richard Rubin at richard.rubin@wsj.com and Olivia Beavers at Olivia.Beavers@wsj.com

data.title: Japan Producer Price Index Edges Higher in March
data.description:
Japan's producer price index rose 4.2% on year in March and gained 0.4% from February, reported the Bank of Japan on Wednesday.

The PPI had risen by a more modest 4.1% on-year in February.

Pushing up the PPI in March were beverage and foods, up 3.1% on year, and petroleum and coal products, up 8.6%, among other items.

Transportation equipment gained 1.8% on year while electric power, gas and water bills rose 6.4% on year in March.

The PPI measures prices at the factory gate, or those charged to businesses and distributors. It is distinct from the consumer price index (CPI), which measures prices in retail locations.

The PPI is considered a precursor to future movements in the CPI, as retailers try to recoup costs.

data.title: South Korea Briefly Halts Kospi Trading After Sharp Gains
data.description:
By Kwanwoo Jun

South Korea's stock-market regulator briefly halted trading on the main exchange Thursday morning after relief over President Trump's tariff pause fueled sharp gains.

The Korea Exchange said in a statement that it activated a "sidecar" limit to suspend trading on the Kospi equities market for five minutes from 9:06am local time. Trading has now resumed.

The trading curb was triggered by the Kospi 200 Futures index's surge of more than 5% earlier, the regulator said. The halt is designed to limit excessive market moves.

The Kospi was last 4.7% higher, coming off earlier gains of more than 5%. The upturn follows Trump's announcement of a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for most trading partners that has sparked a broad-based market rally in South Korea, which faces tariffs that could hurt key exports.

Write to Kwanwoo Jun at kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com

data.title: Shanghai Composite Index Opens 1.3% Higher at 3227.84
Opthea Cuts 65% Staff, Terminates Drug Trials
data.description:
Opthea has terminated phase three clinical trials for two of its drug candidates following negative results, according to a Thursday Australian bourse filing.

The company will be reducing its workforce by about 65% to cut costs while retaining a limited number of employees to ensure compliant termination of clinical trial activities and oversee administrative operations, the filing added.

The company expects the workforce reduction to take effect from May 1 with one-off costs of about $4.5 million, resulting reduction in monthly employee costs of $1 million.

The company sees unaudited cash and cash equivalents of $100 million at the end of March.

The company said its ability to continue as a going concern remains materially uncertain.

data.title: USD/JPY Could Rise to 149.00 as Tariff Fears Ease, Sony Financial SaysMarket Talk
data.description: The dollar could rise to 149.00 yen in Thursday's trading, while continuing to maintain an uptrend as concerns over the impact of higher U.S. tariffs ease, says Sony Financial Group analyst Juntaro Morimoto. The pair is trading at around 146.90. However, "if any negative cues emerge, risk aversion may intensify again," he says. Markets remain nervous as there are still reciprocal tariffs of 10%, and President Trump has said he would impose a 125% tariff on China for retaliating against the U.S. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

2117 ET - The dollar could rise to 149.00 yen in Thursday's trading, while continuing to maintain an uptrend as concerns over the impact of higher U.S. tariffs ease, says Sony Financial Group analyst Juntaro Morimoto. The pair is trading at around 146.90. However, "if any negative cues emerge, risk aversion may intensify again," he says. Markets remain nervous as there are still reciprocal tariffs of 10%, and President Trump has said he would impose a 125% tariff on China for retaliating against the U.S. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

2105 ET - President Trump's 90-day pause on higher U.S. tariffs for most countries is "not as helpful as it sounds," Citi Research's Andrew Hollenhorst says in a research report. "Pausing reciprocal tariffs excluding China doesn't mean the U.S. economy has avoided a slowdown in growth and rise in inflation," the chief U.S. economist says. The U.S.' 10% baseline tariff, additional 125% tariffs on most Chinese goods, and sector-specific tariffs lift the average U.S. effective tariff rate by around 21 percentage points from its level at the start of 2025, he says. "Uncertainty over trade will persist," Hollenhorst adds. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2043 ET - Asian currencies are mixed against the U.S. dollar in the early session as traders digest President Trump's tariff announcements overnight. Trump unveiled a 90-day pause on higher U.S. tariffs for most countries but also increased the tariff on Chinese imports to 125%. "Trump's objectives suggest the global trade war can persist," CBA's Carol Kong says in a research report. "Tariff rates could yet go higher," the economist and currency strategist says. USD/KRW is up 0.6% to 1,455.88, while USD/JPY is down 0.6% to 146.86 and AUD/USD is off 0.4% to 0.6131, according to FactSet. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2005 ET - JGBs slide in price terms in the morning Tokyo session, dragged by risk-on sentiment triggered by President Trump's announcement on Wednesday of a 90-day pause on higher U.S. tariffs for most countries. This tariff reversal has sparked a relief rally in risk assets, two members of Sucden Financial's Research team say in a commentary. Trump's move lessens the impact of the initially aggressive trade policy stance, say the members, adding that bonds have been dumped. The 10-year JGB yield rises 11bps to 1.380%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1946 ET - Japanese stocks may rise as fears about U.S. tariffs ease following President Trump's pause for so-called reciprocal tariffs. The Nikkei futures are up 10% at 35110 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 147.59, up sharply from 145.38 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any responses from China after Trump said he would raise levies on Chinese imports to 125%. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 3.9% to 31714.03 on Wednesday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1736 ET [Dow Jones]--Canada bond yields ended Wednesday higher, fueled by a selloff after Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent initially indicated Canada and Mexico would face a new, broad-based 10% tariff as part of President Trump's amended tariff policy. A US administration official would later clarify that neither Canada nor Mexico would face any new duties other than what was imposed prior to the Liberation Day announcement. For instance, the 10-year Canada government bond yield was trading in the 3.16% range for two hours ahead of Bessent remarks to reporters outside the White House, to elaborate on Trump's decison on a 90-day pause on reciprocal duties. The 10-year Canada yield shot up to roughly 3.25%, and ended the trading session at about 3.2%. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com)

1646 ET [Dow Jones]--Former Bank of Canada deputy governor Paul Beaudry says the 90-day pause on US reciprocal tariffs represents a walk back from "extreme" trade policy. He tells Canadian Broadcasting Corp. that despite the policy change from the White House, uncertainty will remain elevated because the global economy remains in a "high-tariff situation." The key thing to watch, he says, are how households and businesses respond. Furthermore, Beaudry says the trade war between the US and China "is not a small thing" and will have repercussions on the global economy. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com)

1547 ET - Treasury yields maintain early gains after President Trump announced a pause on tariffs. Yields have been rising in past days amid concerns that trade uncertainties could weaken demand of U.S. government debt. A successful auction of 10-year Treasurys today eased some of those fears, briefly pushing down yields. March CPI tomorrow is expected to show inflation cooling slightly, according to a WSJ survey. The 10-year rises 0.151 percentage point to 4.410% and the two-year gains 0.207 p.p. to 3.942%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1527 ET [Dow Jones]--Pausing America's so-called reciprocal tariffs on countries excluding China doesn't mean the U.S. economy has avoided a slowdown and rise in inflation, argues Citi's Andrew Hollenhorst. The economist says the 10% baseline tariff announced during the 90-day pause on reciprocal levies and additional 125% tariffs on most Chinese goods plus sector-specific tariffs still raises the U.S. effective tariff rate by about 21 percentage points from the level at the beginning of the year. And Hollenhorst adds that uncertainty over trade will persist and non-China imports may now surge, damping U.S. growth in 2Q. Citi continues to expect the Fed will cut policy rates in May or June. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

1524 ET [Dow Jones]--There's more uncertainty among traders regarding tariffs on Canada and Mexico. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told reporters that the 10% universal tariff now applies to both Canada and Mexico. That was not the case following the April 2 Liberation Day announcement, as the two USMCA nations were exempt from any new tariffs but still faced 25% tariffs on assembled vehicles, aluminum, steel and other goods not deemed USMCA compliant. Representatives for Canada PM Mark Carney didn't immediately respond to request for comment. Bessent's remarks led to a selloff in Canada government bonds across the curve. The Canadian dollar has retraced some of its earlier gains based on 90-day pause, and the reduced likelihood of a U.S. recession. "Confusion still reigns as to whether the previously announced measures remain in place, so currency markets are rudderless," Karl Schamotta, chief market strategist at forex firm Corpay, tells the WSJ. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com)

1522 ET [Dow Jones]--While President Trump was able to resist the stock market selloff it was only a matter of time before he folded on his high tariffs once the bond market began to weaken too, says Capital Economics' Paul Ashworth. Following Trump's social-media announcement of a pause and temporary reset on so-called reciprocal tariff rates on most countries, Ashworth says the working assumption is now that the president will repeatedly extend the pause and what stays in place is effectively the 10% tariff he campaigned on. In return, the economist expects minor concessions from countries on their own tariffs and trade practices. Still, he says that when it comes to China-U.S. relations, it is difficult to see either side backing down in the next few days but there is likely to be a full rollback of all additional tariffs applied since inauguration day. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

1415 ET - Treasury yields are little changed after Trump's tariff relief, and remain mostly above yesterday levels. A successful auction of 10-year notes calmed bond markets minutes before Trump's social media post announcing a 90-day pause on some tariffs, except for China. Madison Investments' Mike Sanders says solid demand in today's auction was probably due to the recent spike on yields, which made the securities more attractive. He says the tariff relief was to be expected, given the distress they caused in Wall Street. There is a lot of uncertainty still, he says. "We are trading off a social media post," he says. The 10-year yield is at 4.428%, off its morning high. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: National Australia Bank Analysts Now See More Rate Cuts From Reserve Bank in Wake of Trade Turmoil
data.description:
The National Australia Bank's (NAB) economic analysts now expect more aggressive easing from the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) due to the escalation of global trade tensions, according to a Thursday statement.

NAB Economics sees the RBA delivering a 50-basis-point cut in May, followed by 25 basis points at each meeting in July, August, November, and 2026. This will take the official cash rate to 2.6% by February 2026 from the current 4.1%.

"Our call for a 50bps easing in May reflects the fact that with the real cash rate of 1.3% and policy currently restrictive, the RBA needs to play catch up," said NAB Economic Chief Economist Sally Auld.

"Once the cash rate reaches a level more consistent with a neutral policy setting, we then expect the RBA to pause for a few months before taking the cash rate into modestly accommodative territory," Auld added.

data.title: Trump Hikes Tariffs on China to 125%, Pauses on Other Countries
data.description:
US President Donald Trump has further raised duties on Chinese imports to 125% from 104% late Wednesday in the US, hours after China hiked tariffs on US goods to 84%.

Citing "the lack of respect that China has shown to the world's markets," Trump said the new tariff on China is effective immediately.

"At some point, hopefully in the near future, China will realize that the days of ripping off the USA, and other countries is no longer sustainable or acceptable," Trump said on Truth Social.

Meanwhile, Trump has authorized a "90-day pause" on retaliatory tariffs imposed on other countries, saying they would only be subject to a 10% tariff.

"These countries have not, at my strong suggestion, retaliated in any way, shape, or form," the president said.

data.title: Catapult Bull Sees Positives Outweighing Tariff NegativeMarket Talk
data.description: Catapult Sports International's bull at Bell Potter sees the positives outweighing the negatives despite uncertainty over the potential impact of tariffs on the sports-tech provider. Analyst Chris Savage tells clients in a note that he anticipates a strong annual result, with annual contract value rising 19% on-year to US$103 million. He is also positive that Catapult can continue to deliver on cross-selling, especially with new product launches on the way. Savage points out that tariffs will only impact Catapult's hardware business, which is relatively small. Other than the Australian dollar, all currencies in which Catapult does business have held up well against the U.S. dollar, he adds. Bell Potter keeps a buy rating and A$4.00 target price on the stock, which is up 11% at A$3.35. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: China Looks to Advance Talks With EU After Trade Call, Commerce Ministry Says
data.description:
China has pledged to accelerate talks with the European Union on various hot-button issues as Beijing looks to forge closer ties with trading partners in the face of escalating tensions with the U.S.

According to China's commerce ministry, Chinese Commerce Minister Wang Wentao held a video call with the European Commissioner for Trade and Economic Security, Maros Sefcovic, on Tuesday. During the call, the two sides discussed a range of bilateral trade issues--including electric vehicles--as well as President Trump's so-called reciprocal tariffs, the ministry said on Thursday.

Both officials have agreed to start consultations on market access, and to immediately conduct negotiations on EV price commitments, the Chinese ministry said. They also discussed cooperation on auto industry investment, pledging to support the restarting of a trade remedy dialogue mechanism, according to the ministry.

The call happened around the same time as Chinese Premier Li Qiang had a phone call with EU President Ursula von der Leyen, who during the talk mentioned the potential oversupply of cheap Chinese goods to the trading bloc as fresh U.S. tariffs disrupt global trade.

Minister Wang on Wednesday also held a talk with his Malaysian counterpart, Tengku Zafrul Aziz, on trade cooperation and tariff issues, the Chinese commerce ministry said.

Write to Singapore editors at singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: China and EU discuss trade in response to U.S.' punitive tariffs
data.description:
China and the European Union have exchanged views on strengthening their economic and trade cooperation in response to U.S. tariffs, the Chinese Commerce Ministry said on Thursday.

In a video call on Tuesday, China's Commerce Minister Wang Wentao discussed with European trade and economic security commissioner Maros Sefcovic the restart of talks on trade relief and to immediately carry out negotiations on electric vehicle price commitments, the Chinese ministry statement said.

The conversation came shortly before before U.S. President Donald Trump's additional tariffs on China started taking effect.

Trump has on Wednesday said he would temporarily lower the hefty duties he had just imposed on dozens of countries in a stunning reversal. He, however, ramped up pressure on China, threatening to raise tariffs on the world's second largest economy to 125%.

China is ready to deepen trade, investment and industrial cooperation with the European Union, Wang told Sefcovic.

Wang urged China and the EU to jointly safeguard the rules-based multilateral trading system and adhere to trade liberalisation and facilitation, "which will inject more stability and certainty into the world economy and global trade", the ministry statement said.

China and EU also discussed creating a more favourable business environment for enterprises and trade transfer issues.

They will continue to strengthen communication under the World Trade Organization framework and jointly promote WTO reform, the ministry statement said.

The EU had imposed additional tariffs of up to 35.3% on China-made electric vehicles at the end of October after an anti-subsidy investigation, on top of the bloc's standard 10% car import tariffs.

The commerce ministry said last week that the two sides have agreed to restart negotiations on minimum price commitments on Chinese EVs but did not specify when that would resume.

data.title: Singapore Shares Climb 6.6%, Tracking Wall Street's RallyMarket Talk
data.description: Singapore's FTSE Straits Times Index climbs 6.6% to 3617.89 in early trade, tracking Wall Street's rally overnight. President Trump's announcement of a 90-day pause in some of the U.S. reciprocal tariffs on the world has caused a "market that has been under extreme pressure for the past week to explode higher," Phillip Securities Research team says in a commentary. Among the best performers on the benchmark index, SATS Ltd. rises 8.5%, index heavyweight DBS adds 8.2%, and Mapletree Logistics Trust is 8.6% higher. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)
